text: COW-PASTURES, an extensive grazing district in E Australia, about 40 m. to the SW of Sydney, watered by a river called the Cow-Pasture river, which, after its junction with the Warragamby, a stream issuing from the Blue mountains, forms the Nepean.

COWAL, a district of Argyleshire, forming a peninsula or point of land stretching NE and SW, between the frith of Clyde and Loch-Fyne; and comprehending the parishes of Dunoon, Inverchaolin, Kilfinan, Kilmodan, Kilmorich, Lochgoilhead, Strachur, and Strathlachlan. The NE part of the district, which borders with Perthshire, presents a rugged and broken surface. The mountains become gradually lower, and the surface less rugged, as you advance to the SW; and towards the extremity, comparatively speaking, the land is low and level. The hills afford excellent pasture for sheep and black cattle. This district is intersected by three arms of COW the sea, Loch-Ridden, Loch-Streven, and Loch-Eck; and is watered by the rivers Cur and Eachaig. The coast is partly flat, and partly bold and rocky, presenting numerous creeks and small harbours. Pop. in 1831, 7,943; families 1,475. Pop. in 1841, 9,397.
COWALITZ. See COWLITZ.
COWAN, a township in Wayne co., in the state of Missouri. Pop. 432.
COWARNE (LITTLE), a parish in Herefordshire, 41 m. SW by W of Bromyard. Area 830 acres. Pop. 187.
COWARNE (Much), a parish in Herefordshire, 6 m. SW of Bromyard. Area 2,970 acres. Pop. 557.
COWBIT, a parish in the co. of Lincoln, 5 m. NNE of Crowland. Area 4,590 acres. Pop. 664.

COWBRIDGE, a market-town and parish in the co. of Glamorgan, 12 m. W of Cardiff, and 7 m. E by S of Bridgend, on the small river Ddau. In conjunction with Cardiff and Llantrissent, this town sends a member to parliament. The number of electors registered in 1837 was 92; in 1847, 86. The town consists of one broad long street. Pop. in 1801, 759; in 1831, 1,098; in 1841, 1,080.
COWDEN, a parish in Kent, 71 m. W of Tunbridge-Wells. Area 3,170 acres. Pop. 695.
COWDONS, a township in the p. of Mappleton, E. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. SSE of Hornsea. Area 1,750 acres. Pop. 151.

COWER, a trading port of W Africa, on the Gambia, between Tinderbar and Yanimaroo. Its trade is considerable. Corn, millet, hides, coarse country cloths, ground-nuts, ivory, and wax, are here exchanged for rum, tobacco, gunpowder, iron-bars, and coral and amber - beads. There are several large native-towns at a short distance from the port, all of which bear the name of C.

COWES (East), a hamlet in the p. of Whippingham, Isle-of-Wight division of the co. of Southampton, 42 m. N of Newport, on the E bank of the Medina or Cowes river, at its mouth. Pop. 880. From its vicinity to West C., this is a thriving place, and has considerable trade. The custom-house is here, and there are bonded warehouses .- Osborne house, now the marine residence of Her Majesty, is beautifully situated in the neighbourhood of East C., in a fine park, well-stocked with noble timber, and adjoining eastward the grounds of Morris-castle. Very little of the mansion is seen from the high road; but in sailing along the coast, the house appears to be a handsome square edifice, seated at the head of an ample lawn which slopes gently to a valley open to the sea-beach. The whole park, strictly private, extends down to the sea, with good landing-places. The mansion was in the occupation of Eustace Mann, Esq., during the civil wars between Charles I. and his parliament.—East C. castle stands on a hill in the vicinity which commands a view of the Solent, superior in point of beauty to any other in the island.

COWES (WEST), a sea-port and chapelry in the p. of Norwood, Isle-of-Wight division of the co. of Southampton, 111 m. SSE of Southampton, and 41 m. N of Newport, on the W bank of the Medina river, at its mouth, directly opposite East C. The streets are narrow and ill-built; but from the terraced manner in which they rise one above another from the water's edge, they have a singular and picturesque appearance, and many handsome houses have been built in the upper part of the town and the vicinity. The convenience of this town for bathing has of late years made it the resort of much fashionable company. Pop. in 1841, 4,107. C. is the principal port of the island. The harbour is safe and convenient; and the roadstead affords excellent shelter in severe weather, the anchorage being so good that vessels seldom drift with any wind. The registered shipping of this port in 1847 amounted to 9,239 tons. Steamers ply daily between C., Portsmouth, and Southampton. There are also steamers to London. The passage up the channel from off C. to London by steamboat required an average of 28 or 30 hours. The distance may now be run by steamer and by railroad from Southampton in 4 or 5 hours.

COWETA, a county of the state of Georgia, U.S., comprising an area of 532 sq. m., bounded on the NW by Chattahoochee, and on the E by Flint rivers, and intersected by New river, and White Oak and Cedar creeks. Pop. in 1830, 5,003; in 1840, 10,364. Its cap, is Newman.
COWFOLD, a parish of Sussex, 51 m. SSE of Horsham. Area 4,640 acres. Pop. 943.
COWICK, a township in the p. of Snaith, W. R. of Yorkshire. Area 8,970 acres. Pop. 882. The hamlets of East and West Cowick are included in this township.
COWIE, a river of Kincardineshire, which rises on the E side of Wedder Hill, runs SE, passes Ury and a village of the same name, and falls, after a course of 9 or 10 m., into Stonehaven bay.
COWIE. See Kowie.
COWISHAR, a town of Hindostan, in the kingdom of Nepal, 75 m. N of Jemlah, on the l. bank of the Sarjou or Gogra.
COWL. See COEL.
COWLAM, a parish of the E. R. of Yorkshire, 63 m. NNW of Great Driffield. Area 1,930 acres. Pop. 44.

COWLEY, a parish of Gloucestershire, 5 m. S of Cheltenham. Area 1,600 acres. Pop. 317.—Also a parish of Middlesex, 12 m. S of Uxbridge, near the Grand Junction canal. Area 300 acres. Pop. 392. —Also a parish of Oxfordshire, E of the Isis, <math>2\frac{1}{4}</math> m. SSE of Oxford. Area 940 acres. Pop. 606.—Also one of the four quarters of the p. of Gnosall, Staffordshire, 41 m. ENE of Newport.
COWLEY, a small island of the Galapagos group, between Albemarle and James' islands, in S lat. 0° 23', and W long. 91° 5'.

COWLING, a parish of Suffolk, 7 m. NNW of Clare. Area 2,820 acres. Pop. 882.—Also a township in the p. of Kildwick, W. R. of Yorkshire, 5\frac{1}{4} m. SSW of Skipton. Area 5,140 acres. Pop. 2,458. In this township is the hamlet of Cowling Hill, containing 132 inhabitants. See also Burrell-cum-

COWLITZ, or Cowalitz, a river of Oregon, which rises in the Cascade mountains, in about N lat. 46° 30', and flowing S by W, discharges itself into the Columbia river, on the r. bank, 30 m. below the Willamette, and 50 m. from the coast, after a course of 35 m. It is not navigable except during the freshets. About its head-waters lies some of the finest land to the N of the Columbia; and between it and the Chickelees river, is an extensive prairie, known as the Cammas plains.
COWNY, a township in the p. of Llangadfan, Montgomeryshire. Pop. 62.
COWPEN, a township of Horton parochial chapelry, Northumberland, 7 m. SSE of Morpeth, at the mouth of the Blyth.
COWPEN-BEWLEY, a township of the p. of Billingham, co. of Durham, 41 m. NNE of Stocktonupon-Tees. Area 2,590 acres. Pop. 196.
COWPENS, a village of Spartanburg district, in the state of South Carolina, 117 m. NW of Columbia, between Pacolet and Broad rivers.
COWRIAGUNGE, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 86 m. SE of Delhi, and 15 m. ESE of Coel, on the r. bank of the Callee Nuddee.
COWSBY, a parish of the N. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. NNE of Thirsk. Area 1,220 acres. Pop. 108. COX
COWTHORP, a parish of the W. R. of Yorkshire, 3 m. NNE of Wetherley, on the Nidd. Area 1,280 acres. Pop. 115.

COWTON (East), a parish of the N. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. NE of Catterick, on the Great North of England railway. Area 3,150 acres. Pop. 454. Included in this parish are the township of North, and the chapelry of South Cowton; the former 14 m. NE of Catterick. Area 1,030 acres. Pop. 273. The latter 4 m. NE of Catterick. Area 1,530 acres. Pop. 152.
COWVAH, a small river of Siberia, flowing into the sea of Okotsk at Grebay, 20 versts W of Toroinsk.

COX, a town of Spain, in Valencia, in the prov. of Alicante, and on the road from Orihuela to the capital. Pop. 1,172. It is of great antiquity, and contains a parish church, several convents, and a customhouse.
COXILHA-DE-SANTA-ANNA, a mountainrange of Brazil, in the prov. of São-Pedro-do-Rio- Grande.
COX ISLAND, a small island of the South Pacific, in the group of the Friendly islands, in S lat. 17° 30′, and W. long. 179° 25′.

COX'S BAZAAR, a town of Hindostan, in Chittagong, on the Arracan frontier, at the embouchure of the Nauf, on the W coast of the bay of Bengal, in N lat. 21° 18', E long. 92° 20'. It occupies an elevated site, and has an excellent supply of spring-water.
COX'S BIGHT, a deep inlet on the S coast of Van-Diemen's Land, in Slat. 43° 35', E long. 146° 23'.

COX'S CHANNEL, a narrow arm of the sea on the NW coast of America, separating North island from the N coast of Queen Charlotte's islands. The passage at the W entrance is narrowed by a reef and a bank to a breadth of 3 cables; but in the narrowest part the soundings are from 32 to 40 fath.
COXE'S CREEK, a village in Knox co., in the state of Tennessee, 175 m. E of Nashville.
COXE'S GROUP, a group of islands in Hooper Inlet, on the NE coast of America, in N lat. 69° 28', W long. 82° 18', consisting of above 30, and varying in size from 100 yards to 1 m. or more in length.

COX'S PASS, a difficult descent from the Blue Mountains in New South Wales, leading along the left side of Mount York, [alt. 3,292 ft. Lesson,] into the transalpine vale of Clwdd. It is 213 m. from King's Table-land, and 50 m. from Emu.
COX'S POINT, a village of Texas on La Baca bay, an arm of Matagorda bay.

COX'S RIVER, a river of New South Wales, rising in the vale of Clwdd, and flowing E to the Warragamba. The ravines which discharge their waters into this stream present an area of 1,212 sq. m., of which 796 are on the r. or S side of the river in the co. of Westmoreland, and 416 on the l. in the co. of Cook. In the upper part of its valley the river has an alt. of 2,150 ft. above sea-level, and flows over trap and granite.
COX'S STORE, a village in Sampson co., in the state of N Carolina, U. S., 114 m. SSE of Raleigh.

COXEN'S RANGE, an isolated sandstone range in NE Australia, intersected by the parallel of 23° S, to the W of the Isaac's river. From a peak in this range, called by the discoverer, Leichardt, Coxen's peak, the eye wanders southward over an immense flat covered by one uninterrupted mass of forest; to the eastward also the country is flat; but northward it is hilly, range rising behind range to the horizon.
COXHOE, a township in the p. of Kelloe, in the co. and 5½ m. SSE of Durham, at the terminus of the Coxhoe and Clarence railway. Area 870 acres. Pop. 3,904.

COXIM, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Matto Grosso, which descends under the name of the Cama- . puan from the Serra-Cayapos, in the district of Camapuan; bends NE, receiving on the r. the Barreiro, Inferno, Sallado, and Jauru, and on the l. the Paredão, Furado, Orelha-d'Anta, Bicudo, and Taquari-Mirim; and, after a total course of 75 m., unites with the Taquari or Tacoary, in S lat. 18° 24'. Its banks are inhabited by Indians of the Cayapos tribe, and abound with cocoa-palms.
COXLODGE, a township in the p. of Gosforth, Northumberland, 21 m. NNW of Newcastle. Pop. 924.
COXSACKIE, a township in Greene co., in the state of New York, U. S., 22 m. S of Albany. Pop. 3,539.
COXWELL (GREAT), a parish of Berks, 12 m. SW of Great Farringdon. Area 1,440 acres. Pop. 351.
COXWELL (LITTLE), a township in the p. and 14 m. S of Great Farringdon, Berks. Pop. 315.
COXWOLD, a parish and township of the N. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. N of Easingwold. Area 14,590 acres. Pop. 1,076. Area of township, 1,250 acres. Pop. 325.
COYAMA, or COYALMA, a town of New Grenada, in the intendancy of Cundinamarca, prov. of Bogota, 100 m. SW of Santa-Fe-de-Bogota, on the r. bank of the Saldana.
COYBA. See Quibo.
COYCHURCH, a parish of Glamorganshire. Pop. 1,254. It includes the hamlets of Higher and Lower Coychurch. Pop. of former 359, of latter 308.
COYE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Oise, cant. of Creil, 7 m. SW of Senlis. Pop. in 1841, 860. It has extensive manufactories of cordage, thread, lace, &c., and a lime-kiln.

COYLE, a river of Hindostan, which takes its rise in the prov. of Gundwana, in the district and 55 m. E of Sirgoojah, runs NNW between the prov. of Bahar and Gundwana, and discharges itself into the Sone, in E long. 83° 48'. See also BYTURNEY.

COYLTON, a parish in the district of Kyle in Ayrshire, stretching between the Doon and Ayr water. Pop. in 1801, 848; in 1841, 1,484. It comprises the villages of Craighall, Gadgirth-holm, Joppa, and Knockshoggle-holm.
COYTY, a parish of Glamorganshire, 2 m. NE of Bridgend. Pop. 1,930. It includes the hamlets of Higher and Lower Coyty. Pop. of former, 459; of latter, 1,471.
COZ, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, 7 m. NE of Alcobaça. Pop. 1,500.
COZAR, a village of Spain, in the prov. and 39 m. ENE of Ciudad-Real, near the l. bank of the Xabalon.

COZES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, arrond. of Saintes. -The cant. comprises 15 com. Pop. 12,944.—The town is 13 m. SW of Saintes, on the r. bank of the Gironde. Pop. 1,871.
COZUMEL. See COSUMEL.
COZZO, a village of Piedmont, in the prov. of Lomellina, 8 m. SSW of Mortara.

CRAB ISLAND, a small island in the gulf of St. Lawrence, 350 fath. off shore, and a 1 m. NW of Boulet isle.—Also an islet off the coast of British Guayana, in N lat. 6° 24' 30", W long. 57° 22'. It is 8 m. N of the bar of the Berbice river.—Also an island off the W coast of Madagascar, in about S lat. 21° 8'.
CRAB ISLAND. See BIEQUES.
CRAB-BOTTOM, a village in Pendleton co., in the state of Virginia, U.S., 173 m. NW of Richmond.
CRAB-ORCHARD, a village in Lincoln co., in the state of Kentucky, U.S., 62 m. SSE of Frankfort. Pop. 300. II. 10 CRA
CRAB-RUN, a village in Pendleton co., in the state of Virginia, U.S., 151 m. NW of Richmond.
CRACATOA, an islet in the straits of Sunda, in S lat. 6° 9′, E long. 105° 28′.
CRACAU, a village of Saxony, in the circle of Meissen, 15 m. E of Grossenhayn, on the l. bank of the Palsnitz. Pop. 200.
CRACH, a commune of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, cant. of Auray, 15 m. SE of Lorient. Pop. 1,715.
CRACOE, a township in the p. of Burnsall, in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. NNW of Skipton. Area 1,310 acres. Pop. 153.

CRACOW, or Krakow, a small republic of Central Europe, recently extinguished as an independent state, and incorporated with Western Galicia by an ordinance of Austria. It lies between the parallels of 16° 46' and 17° 51' N; and is bounded on the N by Poland; on the E and S by Austrian Galicia; and on the W by Prussia. The Austrian frontier is at the distance of 145 ft., or the width of the river from the capital; Russia approaches it within 10 m.; and the Prussian frontier is 38 m. distant. Its superficial extent is only 22.93 German, or 486 English sq. m.; and consists of a plain running along the N bank of the Vistula, which becomes navigable immediately under the walls of the capital. The Vistula receives within the territory of C. the small rivers Cznerna, Przemza, Radeva, Monuszka, Chobka, and Wolika. A few low ramifications of the Carpathian chain undulate the surface. The climate is temperate, and rather cold than warm; and the soil, which is generally a brown loamy stiff clay, is fertile, and produces an excellent white heavy wheat, flax, maize, good garden-vegetables, and tobacco. Apples, plums, cherries, chestnuts, almonds, and peaches, are reared in the neighbourhood of the capital; but the climate is not genial enough to ripen the grape. Cattle and poultry are extensively reared. Mines of coal, calamine, and zinc are wrought; and a small quantity of alum and iron is produced. The principal manufactures are pottery ware, paper, woollens, leather, brandy, and beer. The peasantry manufacture their own cloth and linen, and a few weavers supply the capital. The annual value of the exports from this territory was some years ago estimated at about £45,000; the imports, at £65,000. The population is chiefly of Polish extraction, and the Polish language is everywhere dominant. The pop. in 1819 was estimated at 95,822; in 1843, at 145,787. In 1815, when the final fate of Poland was decided at the congress of Vienna, the Austrian and Russian monarchs respectively laid claim to the little town and territory of C., situated at the very point where the newly acquired territories of these two powers and those of Prussia join together. To this lucky position C. owed its brief exemption from the fate of Poland. The holy allies, unable to determine which of their number had the best right to this little territory, magnanimously resolved that none of their number should possess it, and finally declared C. a republic under the protection of the three surrounding powers, by the two following articles of the treaty of Vienna: "Art. 6.-The city of Cracow, with its territory, is declared in perpetuity a free, independent, and strictly neutral city, under the protection of Russia, Prussia, and Austria. "Art. 9.—The courts of Russia, Austria, and Prussia engage to respect, and to cause to be respected for ever, the neutrality of the free city of Cracow and of its entire territory. No armed force can ever be introduced into it, under any pretence whatsoever." The constitution of the republic as thus guaranteed placed the legislative power in the hands of a popular representative assembly, which met towards the close of each year, the sittings never exceeding 4 weeks. On these occasions laws were discussed, the administration reviewed, and the budget drawn up. This assembly likewise elected the senators and magistrates, who were responsible to it. Each community, or parish, sent a deputy to the assembly, which likewise contained 3 members from the senate, 3 prelates from the chapter, 3 doctors from the faculties of the university, and 6 judges in equity. The president was chosen from the 3 members sent by the senate. No change of any existing laws could be proposed in this assembly which had not previously received the sanction of the senate. The executive power was vested in the senate, which consisted of a president and 12 members, who were required to be 35 years of age, and to contribute at least 150 Polish florins to the public revenue. The president, and 8 senators, were elected by the popular representatives, the university elected 2, and the chapter the other 2. Six of the former class of senators, and one of each of the latter, retained their office for life; the others were changed annually. The president was elected every three years, but might be re-elected. The senate exercised the patronage of the republic.—The political divisions of C. were into town and country communities; the former containing at least 2,000, and the latter 2,500 inhabitants. Every community was governed by a starost, or mayor; and in every district of 6,000 inhabitants was a judge of equity, a kind of justice-of-peace. The electors included all who paid 50 florins to the public service, or belonged to a liberal profession.—The military force consisted of a town-militia, and a body of gens-d'armes for the preservation of public order. In 1836-7, the revenue was 1,775,766 florins, or £44,500. In 1845, the budget amounted to 2,231,343 florins.—The 26 communes of the C. territory contained 1 town, 2 boroughs, and 77 villages and hamlets. In 1835, the three protecting powers took military possession of the territory of C., and virtually annihilated its existence as an independent state. But its final confiscation to Austria was only effected in 1846. In vindication of this act it was alleged that the independence and neutrality of the little republic had been guaranteed by the great powers only on the condition of the strictest neutrality on the part of that state; that the guarantee of Europe was not, therefore, an absolute, but a relative guarantee; that the republic of C. compromised its independence by breaking that neutrality, which was a sine qua non condition; that not content with proclaiming the revolution, and turning out the authorities established by the treaty of Vienna, C. carried the revolution into the territory of the protecting powers; that the inhabitants of C. sent armed bands into Galicia, and propagated their revolutionary ideas in the grand-duchy of Posen and in the provinces of Poland. Austria, it was further argued, "though attacked on her own territory, preferred preserving her character of protective power than to make reprisals as she might have done; and only consented to the incorporation of C. in her dominions, because Russia proposed the alternative to the two powers to take that step, else Russia would take C. herself." Finally, the Austrian manifesto describes the independence of C. as the joint gift of the three powers; and argues that, upon the revocation of the treaties conferring its independence, the territory must lapse again to its former possessor as before 1809. To these arguments and representations it was replied, that "Each clause in this recital contains a suppression or misrepresentation of the truth. It is obviously monstrous in viewing the question to exclude all considerations of Poland before the year 1809. The paper notoriously falsifies and ludicrously exaggerates the events and their consequences by which the neutrality—the alleged condition of the city's existence—is shown to have been broken. But the great and cardinal falsehood of the whole is the assertion that the independence of C. rested solely on the gift of the three powers, was held solely by their sufferance, and was revocable always at their caprice. That these private stipulations of the three powers, as regarded C., were subsequently incorporated in the great treaty of Vienna was a fact too notorious to be concealed, though an open and unblushing denial of it would hardly have been a more insulting act to the other contracting parties than such a nullification of their interest and influence in the original deed as is here put forth. 'Inasmuch as the conventions,' says the manifesto, 'relating to C., between the three Powers have only been repeated in the 6th, 7th, 8th, 9th, and 10th articles of the general act of the congress of Vienna, of 9th June, 1815, in order that this act should embrace the different results of the arrangements concluded during the private negociations 722 between the cabinets, and that consequently if the three governments at present change with respect to C. an order of things upon which they voluntarily agreed in the year 1815, they merely exercise an undoubted right, therefore, &c. Now, this, the last clause of a long preamble to the resolutions of the three courts, is all the consideration or notice bestowed upon the point on which the whole question turns; and we ask our readers if they ever saw a more obvious right set aside by a more insolent assumption? No doubt the act of Vienna was intended to 'embrace the different results of the private negociations;' but to what purpose? Was it not to confer on these results the very character which they possessed not before, and which is now denied? For what reason were the conditions of C.'s existence rehearsed in the general treaty, and sanctioned by the eight great powers, if not for the establishment of them in a corresponding position? If the independence of C. dates only from the 3d of May, what imaginable ground was there for mooting the subject at all on the 9th of June? What change was it conceived that the previous 'private negociations between the cabinets' were to undergo by their being 'embraced in the general act?' At some instance and for some purpose they certainly were brought forward—at whose? and for what? Is it not glaringly evident that the agreement of the three partitioning parties as to the portion of their spoil which should belong to none of them was a matter of private negociation; but that when the substance of this agreement was introduced into a subsequent and general compact between themselves and five other powers, it became a matter depending upon the fiat of the whole eight? Nothing can be clearer than that the private stipulations of the 3d of May were superseded by the general compact of the 9th of June, to which, in fact, in this part of it, they served as an introduction. The protecting powers were no longer Russia, Austria, and Prussia, but England, Russia, Austria, Prussia, France, Spain, Portugal, and Sweden. Under their fiat the provisional existence of C. became permanently established; and if there were any conditions attached to the concession, it was for these powers to judge of their violation." [Times.] A proclamation declaring the treaty of 3d May 1815 to be "abrogated," and that it had been decreed that "C. together with its territory is to be given back to Austria, and to be incorporated in the Austrian monarchy," to be held as before the year 1809, appeared on the 16th of November 1846. By subsequent arrangements the city of C. was declared to be the capital of the newly created government of West Galicia; and the circles of Bochnia, Rzeszois, and Joslav, were separated from East Galicia, and formed with the territory of C. into the new government. It is stated that Austria, in return for the cession of C., made over to Prussia the lordship of Hotzenplotz, a district in Austrian Silesia, and to Russia a portion of territory in the N part of the Galician circle of Tarnopol. See Galicia. Cracow, the capital of the quondam republic of that name, is situated at the foot of Mount Krakus or Wavel, [alt. 699 ft. above sea-level,] in the extensive valley of the Vistula, and on the l. bank of that river at its confluence with the Radeva, in N lat. 50° 3' 52", E long. 19° 55'. It is surrounded on three sides by hills; the St. Bronislava, on which a monumental mound, rising from a base 300 ft. in diam., to the height of 175 ft., is erected in memory of Kosciusko; the Krakus, and the Wanda; and is united, on the S, to the town of Podgorze or Josephstadt, on the opposite side of the river, by a bridge of rafts 145 ft. long. Approaching C. from Warsaw, nothing meets the eye but a large green valley, fertile as the Touraine in France, and strewed with trees. The Vistula waters this valley, meandering in autumn amongst golden crops of grain; and in the S horizon are seen the varied lines of those great chains of mountains which spread from the Black sea to the Danube. In the middle of this valley, surrounded by numerous villas and convents, fertile meadows and blooming gardens, rise the Gothic pinnacles of the churches of C., and the towers of its castle. The town is surrounded with promenades shaded with tall poplars, which have replaced the old shattered ramparts, walls, and towers. It consists of several distinct quarters, the chief of which are C., Stradom, and Kazimierz. The last named quarter, which lies on an island in the Vistula, and is joined to the town by a bridge, is the residence of the Jews, who have a synagogue here. It is indeed a peculiarity of C. that all its quarters bear different names, and form indeed separate little towns, each of which at one time had its own government. The suburbs are extensive. The streets of C. are most of them tortuous and dark; the houses have festooned gables like those of Augsburg or Nuremburg. Here we see gates adorned with small columns and covered by vines, as they sometimes are in the villages along the banks of the Rhine; while castle, donjon, Gothic church, and Italian façade, are strangely and picturesquely intermingled. On all sides peaked towers, and gilded crosses, and statues, meet the eye. There are no fewer than 38 churches in C.; some remarkable for their architecture, others for their pious traditions, but many of them in ruins; indeed almost every building bears striking marks of ruined grandeur: the old palace of Sobieski is now cut down into shops, and many large private residences are uninhabited and falling into ruins. That of Notre-Dame dates from the 13th cent., and contains a great number of marble altars. The church of St. Peter and St. Paul was reconstructed by Sigismund III., after the model of St. Peter at Rome; that of the Dominicans, founded in 1230, has a double row of stalls of sculptured oak. St. Anne's church possesses a modern monument to the memory of Copernicus, who, it is alleged, was a Cracovian by birth. But the most striking of the ancient monuments of C. is the cathedral, a beautiful specimen of Gothic architecture, and the finest in Poland. This superb edifice was destroyed by fire, but rebuilt by Nanker, bishop of C., in 1320. Here the kings of Poland were crowned. It is rich in gold and silver ornaments; and has 50 altars, and above 20 chapels, and contains the tombs of most of the Polish monarchs, of Casimir, John III., Sobieski, St. Stanislaus—whose remains are enclosed in a silver coffin-Prince Poniatowski, Kosciusko, Dombrowski, &c. The archives and library preserved in this edifice contain many valuable manuscripts. -In the centre of the city, upon the Wavel rock, looking down upon the distant plain, rises the old castle of the kings of Poland, rebuilt by Casimir the Great, enriched by his successors, and devastated by the Austrians. It is a spacious Gothic structure, the first building of which was, it is said, the work of Krakus, the founder of the Polish monarchy, about the year 700. It has suffered by two great conflagrations; but was restored to its pristine splendour by Augustus II. It was fortified by Doumourier in 1768, and repaired when in possession of the Austrians, who converted it into barracks. Its subterranean vaults-excavated in the mountainwere formerly the depository of the royal regalia of Poland. A broad and handsome road, "resembling that which leads to the castle of Edinburgh," leads up from the town to the castle, and the summit of the Wavel commands a noble view of the city, the river, and the distant Carpathians. In ascending the staircases, and traversing the galleries of this castle, we find no traces remaining of the ornaments described in such glowing terms of admiration by travellers of the 17th cent.; but its thick walls, and its old towers, still give it an imposing appearance, and the recollection of its former heroic dwellers have stamped on it a character of sublimity. This castle has seen six powerful dynasties pass beneath its roofs. The descendants of the great Gustavus Vasa received here the ensigns of royalty; afterwards the descendants of the electors of Saxony; then the noble Stanislaus Lescyznski; and finally the lover of the empress Catherine. The royal castle of the Jagellons and of the Piasts is now only an Austrian barrack. The university—a short time since one of the richest in Europe-now only contains about 70 students. Natural history, chemistry, theology, and civil law, are taught in it. The library possesses 30,000 vols., and 4,500 MSS.; and it has two museums, one of mineralogy, the other of natural history. From the heights of the Wavel are to be seen, at three points of the horizon, three gigantic tumuli, similar to those near Upsala, which bear the names of the three Scandina- * CRA vian gods. The first of these contains the remains of Cracus; the second those of Wanda, his heroic daughter; and the third, raised in recent times by the pious love of a whole people, is consecrated to the memory of Kosciusko.—There are besides in C. a Piarist college, and several normal and various elementary schools, 5 hospitals, and an orphan asylum. -The pop. of C. in 1843 was 42,900. Coxe estimated the pop. in 1778 at only 16,000; and states that, exclusive of the suburbs, the town contained in that year only 8,894 souls. In the 14th and 15th cents., it contained a pop. of 80,000. Of the present pop. about 20,000 are Jews, and 600 Protestants. There are no manufactures in C. except a few of cloth and woollens. The trade—which is principally in the hands of the Jews-is not extensive; although C. is the chief depot of Hungarian wines, salt, and wax, and the central point of commerce between Poland and part of Galicia and Hungary. The wines of Hungary, the linens of Silesia, the salt of Vieliczka, and the wax and honey of Galicia, still pass through it. Among the minor branches of traffic is that of eggs, the harvest of the poultry-yards of the Galician peasants. It has always been the depot of considerable quantities of English merchandise, sent hither by the Black sea, Moldavia, Galicia, and even Trieste, and which afterwards find their way into the surrounding countries. It is on the grand Austrian trunk railway line from Vienna to Poland, which runs through the valley of the Oder, while the line between Frankfort and Leipzig connects the valley of the Oder with the Rhine at Biberach; and there is now a line of electrotelegraphic communication between C. and Trieste, a distance of 700 m.—The village of Vieliczk, celebrated for its salt-mines, is only 4 m. distant.—On the 19th of July 1850, C. was ravaged by a destructive fire, supposed to be the work of wilful incendiaries, by which upwards of 300 houses were consumed, with the Episcopal palace, the printing-office of the university, two churches, and the polytechnic school.

CRADLEY, a chapelry and village in the p. and 3 m. NW of Hales-Owen, in Worcestershire. Pop. 2,686. The v. is pleasantly situated on the Stour.— Also a parish in Herefordshire, 7 m. NNE of Ledbury. Area, with that of the adjacent p. of West C., 6,460 acres. Pop. of both 1,504.

CRADOCK, a district of Cape colony, in S Africa; bounded by the river of the same name on the N; Caffraria on the E; the co. of Somerset on the S; and Graaf-Reynet on the W.—The river C. has its head-streams in the Quathlamba mountains. The Stockenstoom's river flowing SW, uniting with the Stormberg river flowing N, in about S lat. 30° 40', forms the main stream of the C., which flows W to the meridian of 26°, and then pursues a NW course to the l. bank of the Gariep or Orange river, which it joins under the parallel of 29° 10', at a point 30 m. SW of Griqua-town. Near its junction with the Gariep it is as fine a river as the latter, flowing between well-wooded banks, and generally about 100 yards wide.

CRADOO, a town of SW Africa, in the kingdom of Jaboo, on the N shore of an extensive lagune of the same name. It formerly carried on an extensive trade in slaves and ivory.—The lagune of C. enters from the bight of Benin by a channel about a 1 m. in breadth, and extends, in a direction parallel to the coast, about 45 m. from W to E. Its greatest breadth is 6 m. When the periodic accumulation of the freshes in the lagunes on this part of the coast find an outlet to the sea, they produce a surf on the bar of Lagos with which a vessel could not safely contend. In the dry season of December, January, and February, the attempt is less formidable. The lagune pours out at half-ebb a volume of surface-scum of a deep brown tint and a sickening odour. Its waters are highest from the month of April until October.
CRAFTON, a village of Pennsylvania co., in the state of Virginia, U. S., 167 m. SW of Richmond.

CRAFTSBURY, a township of Orleans co., in the state of Vermont, U. S., 33 m. NE of Montpelier, watered by Black and Lamoille rivers. Pop. 1,151. The village is finely situated, and is very flourishing.
CRAGGIE (LOCH), a fresh water loch in the p. of Tongue, Sutherlandshire, which, with Lochs Loyal and Slam, discharges itself by the Borgie into Torrisdale bay.

CRAIG, a parish on the coast of Forfarshire. Area 3,308 Scots acres. The surface is undulating, and in several places dotted with small lakes. coast is rocky, and toward the S precipitous. villages are Rossie island, Usan, and Ferryden. Pop. in 1801, 1,328; in 1841, 1,945.
CRAIG, a township of Switzerland co., in the state of Indiana, U.S. Pop. 1,306.
CRAIGHALL, a village in the p. of Inveresk, Edinburghshire. Pop. 501.—Also a v. in the p. of Coylton, in Ayrshire. Pop. 100.
CRAIGHALL (NEW), a village in the parishes of Inveresk and Liberton, in Edinburghshire. Pop. 336.
CRAIGIE, a parish in the district of Kyle, Ayrshire. Coal is worked here, and limestone abounds. Pop. in 1801, 786; in 1841, 779.
CRAIGIE, a village 1 m. S of Perth, in the East church p. Pop. 219.—Also a v. in the p. of Dalmeny, in Linlithgowshire. Pop. 75. *

CRAIGNISH, a parish on the coast of Argyleshire, opposite the gulf of Corrievrekin. Part of it is peninsular, stretching S between Loch C. and the sea, and terminating in C. point. Around it, or embosomed in the arm of the sea which indents it and bears its name, are upwards of 20 islands, rocks, and islets. Pop. in 1801, 904; in 1841, 873.
CRAIGO, a village in the p. of Logie-Pert, in Forfarshire. Pop. 359.
CRAIG-OWL, one of the Sidlaw hills, in the p. of Tealing, Forfarshire. It rises to 1,600 ft. above sealevel, and is the highest summit in the range.
CRAIGSVILLE, a village of Blooming Grove township, Orange co., in the state of New York, U.S., 99 m. SW of Albany.
CRAIGTON, a village in the p. of Monkie, in Forfarshire. Pop. 162.
CRAIKE, a detached parish of the co. of Durham, locally situated in Yorkshire, 21 m. NE of Easingwold. Area 3,300 acres. Pop. 579.

CRAIL, a parish forming the SE extremity of Fifeshire, stretching 6 m. E to Fifeness. Pop. in 1801, 1,652; in 1841, 1,737.—The burgh of C., 2 m. W of Fifeness, and 10 m. SE of St. Andrew's, has a small port. Pop. 1,221. It unites with Cupar, St. Andrew's, Kilrenny, Anstruther, and Pittenweem, in returning a member to parliament.

CRAILING, a parish in Roxburghshire, intersected by the Teviot. Pop. 667, of whom 74 were in the v. of C., which is situated on the Oxnam; and 320 in the v. of East and West Nisbet. CRAILSHEIM, CREILSHEIM, or KRAILSHEIM, a town of Wurtemberg, cap. of a bailiwick, in the circle and on the r. bank of the Jaxt, 13 m. NNW of Elwangen. Pop. in 1840, 3,008. It has a castle, and possesses important manufactures, consisting chiefly in hosiery, calico, printed goods, leather, and jewellery. It has also extensive breweries. The trade consists principally in cattle.
CRAKEHALL, a township in the p. of Bedale, N. R. of Yorkshire, 13 m. NW of Bedale. Area 1,920 acres. Pop. 576.
CRAKENTHORPE, a township in the p. of Bon- CRA gate, in Westmoreland, 3 m. NW of Appleby. Pop. 104.
CRAMAHE, a township in Upper Canada, in the Newcastle district, skirted on the S by Lake Ontario. Pop. 3,200 in 1842.
CRAMANTAN, an estuary of the island of Borneo, on the S coast, SE of Benjar Masseen.
CRAMAUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Tarn, cant. of Monesties. Pop. 1,765. There are large glass-works here.
CRAMBE, a parish in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. SSW of New Malton. Area 3,710 acres. Pop. 610.

CRAMIMUAN, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, which descends from the Cordilheira dos Ai- The mores, runs along the base of Monte-Pascoal, and The discharges itself into a small bay of the Atlantic, 30 m. S of Porto-Seguro.
CRAMLINGTON, a parish and village in Northumberland, 9 m. NNE of Newcastle. Area 720 acres. Pop. 2,634. There are large coal-works here.
CRAMOISY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Oise, cant. of Creil. Pop. 471.

CRAMOND, a parish, partly in Linlithgowshire, partly in Edinburghshire, intersected by the Almond river, and containing the villages of C. and Muttonhole. Pop. of p. in 1801, 1,411; in 1841, 1,981, of whom only 96 were in that part of the p. which is in Linlithgowshire. The v. of C. is 51 m. W of Edinburgh.
CRANAGE, a township in the p. of Sandbach, in Cheshire, 4 m. ENE of Middlewich. Area 2,040 acres. Pop. 512.

CRANBERRY, a village in Middlesex co., in the state of New Jersey, U. S., 22 m. NE of Trenton. Pop. 500.—Also a township in Butler co., in Pennsylvania, 11 m. SW of Butler. Pop. 1,822.—Also a township in Crawford co., in Ohio. Pop. 679.—Also a township in Venango co., in Pennsylvania, 207 m. WNW of Harrisburg. Pop. 942.

CRANBORNE, a parish and market-town of Dorset, 9 m. NNE of Wimborne-Minster. Area of p. 13,730 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,402; in 1841, 2,551.— The town is pleasantly situated in the centre of a rich agricultural district. It was the birth-place of Bishop Stillingfleet.

CRANBROOKE, a parish and market-town of Kent. The p., comprising 10,460 acres, had a pop. in 1841 of 3,996. The town, 71 m. NW by W of Tenterden, on a small brook called the Crane, was founded by a colony of Flemings in the reign of Edward III., and was the first site of the woollen manufacture in England, but has long ceased to be the centre of that important trade. It has a large hopmarket. William Huntington, the founder of a wellknown religious sect of the present day, was a native of this p.
CRANE, a township in the p. of Llanerfyl, Montgomeryshire. Pop. 336.
CRANESVILLE, a village of Daviess co., in the state of Missouri, U.S. Pop. in 1840, 1,269.

CRANFIELD, a parish of Bedfordshire, 6 m. WNW of Ampthill. Area 3,500 acres. Pop. 1,371. -Also a p. of co. and 6 m. W of Antrim. Area 2,691 acres. Pop. 367.—Also a headland of co. Down, on the N side of Carlingford bay.
CRANFORD, a parish of Middlesex, 23 m. NW of Hounslow. Area 790 acres. Pop. 370.
CRANFORD (St. Andrew and St. John), two parishes of Northamptonshire, 31 m. E of Kettering. United area 2,420 acres. Pop. of former 257; of latter 341.

CRANGANORE, or KODUNGALUN, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malabar, on the N bank of a small bay or estuary, 20 m. N of Cochin, and 83 m. SSE of Calicut, in N lat. 10° 13'. It is the resi- | Pop. in 1846, 1,003. The environs produce good dence of a Catholic archbishop, and has several Catholic and Nestorian churches. The port is small, but possesses an active commerce. It is asserted by the Jews that they were in possession of C. so early as the year 490. In 1505 it fell into the hands of the Portuguese, and in 1663 was taken and fortified by the Dutch. Towards the close of the 18th cent. it was sold to the rajah of Travancore, and in 1790 was taken from Tippu by the British.
CRANGE', a numerous tribe of Indians, of the Macamecrans family, who inhabit the woods near the banks of the Tocantins, in the Brazilian provs. of Maranhao and Goyaz.

CRANHAM, a parish of Essex, 21 m. SE of Hornchurch, bounded on the W by the Ingerburn, and near the Thames-Haven railway. Area 1,880 acres. Pop. 280.—Also a p. of Gloucestershire, 21 m. NNE of Painswick. Area 1,970 acres. Pop. 428.
CRANLEY, a parish of Surrey, 7 m. ESE of Godalming, and near the Arun and Wey canal. Area 7,420 acres. Pop. 1,357.
CRANMORE (East), a parish of Somerset, 4 m. E of Shepton-Mallet. Area 980 acres. Pop. 66.
CRANMORE (West), a parish of Somerset, 2 m. E of Shepton - Mallet. Area 2,160 acres. Pop. 319.
CRANOE, a parish of Leicestershire, 5\frac{3}{4} m. NNE of Market-Harborough. Area 990 acres. Pop. 137.
CRANSAC, a town of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of St. Aubin, 19 m. NE of Villefranche. Pop. 473. It is noted for its mineral waters.
CRANSFORD, a parish of Suffolk, 2 m. NE of Framlington, W of the Adde. Area 2,180 acres. Pop. 303.
CRANSHAWS, or Cranshaw, a parish of Berwickshire, consisting of two detached parts, both lying upon the Lammermuir range of hills. Pop. in 1801, 166; in 1841, 120.
CRANSLEY, a parish of Northamptonshire, 3 m. SW of Kettering. Area 2,510 acres. Pop. 319. It includes the hamlet of Little Cransley. Pop. 78.

CRANSTON, a parish of Mid-Lothian, on the SE skirts of the co., intersected from S to N by the Tyne. Pop. in 1801, 895; in 1841, 1,128, of whom 284 were in the vs. of Chesterhill and Sauchenside, and 226 in the v. of Cousland.

CRANSTON, a township of Providence co., in the state of Rhode Island, U. S., 5 m. SW of Providence, watered by Pawtuxet and Powchasset rivers. It has a rugged surface, but is in some parts fertile and well cultivated. Pop. 2,962.
CRANTOCK, a parish of Cornwall, 7 m. NW of St. Michael. Area 2,480 acres. Pop. 450.
CRANWELL, a parish of Lincolnshire, 4 m. NW of Sleaford, near the post-road. Area 2,480 acres. Pop. 230.
CRANWICK, a parish of Norfolk, 61 m. SE of Stoke-Ferry, on the S bank of the Wissey. Area 1,550 acres. Pop. 108.
CRANWORTH, a parish of Norfolk, 53 m. NE of Watton, on the Blackwater. Area 1,100 acres. Pop. 340.

CRAON, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, arrond. of Chateau-Gonthier. The cant. comprises 13 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,006; in 1841, 13,157. The town is pleasantly situated on the l. bank of the Oudin, 13 m. W of Chateau-Gonthier. Pop. in 1846, 3,983. It has a fine castle, and possesses an active trade in grain, cattle, lint, thread, and linen. Fairs are held six times a-year. It is noted as the birth-place of Volney.
CRAONNE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Aisne, arrond. of Laon. The cant. comprises 40 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,765; in 1841, 13,584. The town is 13 m. SE of Laon. CRA wine.

CRAPONNE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Loire, arrond. of Le Puy. The cant. comprises 6 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,080; in 1841, 9,763. The town is situated 22 m. N of Le Puy. Pop. in 1841, 3,763. Its manufactures consist chiefly in lace and cloth. Fairs are held three times a-year.—Also a canal in the dep. of the Bouches-du-Rhone, extending from the Durance in the vicinity of La Roque-d'Antheron to Lamanon, where it divides into 2 branches, one of which terminates in the Etang-de-Berre, and the other at the Rhone, near Arles. It is not navigable.
CRAS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Lot, cant. of Lauzès. Pop. 1,986. It contains the ruins of a Roman camp.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Ain, cant. of Montrevel. Pop. 1,251.
CRASSI, or Crassier, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, district and 4 m. WSW of Nyon, on the Boiron, by which it is separated from France. Pop. 190.
CRASSWALL, a chapelry in the p. of Clodock, Herefordshire, 7 m. SE of Hay, in the co. of Brecon. Pop. 374.
CRASTER, a township in the p. of Embleton, Northumberland, 6 m. NE of Alnwick. Pop. 247.
CRATHORNE, a parish of the N. R. of Yorkshire, 31 m. SSE of Yarm, on a branch of the Tees. Area 2,460 acres. Pop. 294.

CRATHY AND BRAEMAR, an extensive parish, situated in that district of Aberdeenshire called Marr, and supposed to be more elevated above the level of the sea, and farther removed in every direction from the coast, than any other parochial district in Scotland. The length of the inhabited part is about 30 m.; the breadth varies from 8 to 10; but, taking in the mountainous and waste district, the whole district is upwards of 40 m. in length, and 20 m. in breadth. It contains according to Robertson's survey 199,658 acres. By far the greater part is covered with mountains,—some of which are the highest in Scotland, with the exception of Bennevis: the highest are Loch-na-Gar [3,815 ft.], Bennabuird [3,940 ft.], and Benmacdhu [4,390 ft.]. Nearly the whole of C. and B. was originally covered with wood, which was called the forest of Marr; and constituted a part of the Great Caledonian forest. Besides the natural wood, there are extensive plantations of fir and larch. The Dee takes its rise in the forest of B. at an alt. of 4,060 ft. above sea-level, and runs through the whole extent of this district. The principal lakes are Loch-Callader and Loch-Brotachan, which contain trout, salmon, and eels. Glen Callader is interesting to geologists from its numerous displays of the association of granite with slaty primitive rocks. The great military road from Blairgowrie to Fort- George passes through the whole extent of the district. The village of Castleton-of-Braemar is situated on this line of road. Pop. in 1801, 1,876; in 1831, 1,808; in 1841, 1,712.

CRATI, a river of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria Citra, which takes ats rise in the Sila or Brettian forest, in the district and some miles S of Cosenza, cant. and 5 m. E of Aprigliano; passes Cosenza; pursues a N direction to Tarsia when it turns NE; receives the Bussento and Coscile; and after a total course of 60 m. discharges itself into the gulf of Tarento, 14 m. NW of Rossano. The waters of this river, the ancient Crathis, were formerly noted for their medical properties.
CRATIUS, a ramification of the mountains of Cairiris, in Brazil, on the confines of the prov. of Ceara and Piauhi.

CRATO, a town of Portugal, cap. of a comarca, in the prov. of Alemtejo, 15 m. WNW of Portalegre, on a hill, near the N bank of the Ervedal. Pop. 3,000. It is surrounded by walls, and has a citadel, now greatly dilapidated. It is the residence of the grandprior of Malta, and contains a church, a convent, an hospital, and an alms-house.

CRATO, a comarca, district, and town of Brazil, in the prov. of Ceara. Pop. of com. in 1821, 6,975; in 1845, 10,000. The town is pleasantly situated at the confluence of a small river of the same name, with the Salgado, 190 m. S of Ceara. Pop. 3,000. It contains a handsome church and two schools. The environs are extensively irrigated, and produce maize, mandioc, &c., in great abundance.—Also a town of the prov. of Para, on the l. bank of the Madeira, 210 m. SSW of Borba. It is commonly touched at by vessels ascending the Madeira. The environs are fertile, and produce cocoa-nuts, sarsaparilla, and cloves. CRAU (LA)—the Campi Lapidei of the Romans— a singularly stony district of France, in the dep. of the Bouches-du-Rhone, comprising an area of upwards of 120 sq. m., between the canal of Craponne, the etangs de Baux and de Berre, and the Rhône. See BOUCHES-DU-RHONE.
CRAULA, a village of Coburg-Gotha, NW of Gotha. Pop. 421.
CRAVAGLIANA, a commune of Piedmont, in the prov. of Valsesia, 7 m. NW of Varallo. Pop. 1,300.

CRAVANT, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Yonne, cant. of Vermenton, 11 m. SE of Auxerre, at the confluence of the Aire with the Yonne. Pop. in 1846, 1,000. The environs afford good wine.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Loiret, cant. of Beaugency, 19 m. WSW of Orleans. Pop. 1,368.
CRAVEGNA, a town of the Sardinian states, in the prov. and 8 m. N of Domo-d'Ossola. Pop. 500.
CRAVEN, a county in the SE part of N Carolina, U. S., intersected by Neuse river. Pop. 13,438. Its cap. is New Berne.
